comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Once again, it's technically Monday now -- past midnight Eastern time -- and here I am on Dreamwidth, so it's time for another Magic Monday.  Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed. See you next Monday!***

comments.title: Animals
comments.content: Are there any resources that revolve around  the ability of talking to animals such as horses, dogs & birds?

replies.title: Re: Animals
replies.content: Not in the occult traditions known to me, no.

comments.title: Saturn Return, and some follow-up
comments.content:
Many thanks JMG and Christine S for your comments last week on my horoscope. While pondering your suggestions, I’m still trying to work out my best course of action, and would be interested in your further remarks, if any. Here are some features which I previously omitted for the sake of brevity and focus.

Christine recommended that I look to the sign in which is located the cusp of the Second House, and the planet that rules it, as a guide to remunerative occupation. That sign is Aquarius. Chiron is in both sign and house. Also, the cusp of the Second is only just within Aquarius, about a quarter degree past Pisces.

I gather Saturn will be involved here, with perhaps a hint of influence from Jupiter and Venus. I find it hard to imagine how these might combine harmoniously.

Pluto, as I mentioned before, is in the Ninth, in Virgo, and is conjunct the Part of Fortune to within 4 degrees. He is supported by trine to Saturn and to the Sun, both of which are strongly placed by sign, and both in angular houses.

One site suggests that this Pluto placement offers me a strong possibility of going abroad, a thing I desire. Does that appear to you to be the case?

My second Saturn return is right now. It’s proving to be every bit as much fun and light-hearted frivolity as the astrologers all promised it would be. Just now I’m experiencing much the same optimism and buoyancy of spirits as you might expect to find in, say, Marvin the paranoid android.

Kevin

replies.title: Re: Saturn Return, and some follow-up
replies.content:
If your natal Saturn is angular and well placed by sign, he's your friend, not your enemy, and your Saturn return is a good time to buckle down to hard work on yourself and your life, knowing that you can get thirty years of momentum out of the experience. If you look at the sign and the house Saturn is in, you can use that as a guide to a suitable job.

I don't use Chiron or any of the other asteroids, so can't help you with that, and I wouldn't take Pluto in the ninth as a sign of foreign travel.

replies.title: Re: Saturn Return, and some follow-up
replies.content:
Saturn is opposed by the lesser malefic (which is severely afflicted) and is retrograde, but I suppose his placement keeps him strong enough. It’s just as well I’ve no wish to be a soldier.

Do you agree with Ficino’s association of Saturn with astrologers and astronomers? Perhaps I might wind up making star maps or astrolabes. I’d probably enjoy that more than, say, making coffins.

Kevin

replies.title: Re: Saturn Return, and some follow-up
replies.content: Hmm. That makes for a rougher relationship with Saturn, though if you rely on patience and hard work you'll do well. Making star maps and astrolabes is a Venus/Mercury activity, not a Saturn one -- it's the activity that counts, not the clientele. (A portrait painter who specialized in painting pictures of successful undertakers would still be engaged in a Venus activity, as Venus rules the arts.)

replies.title: Re: Saturn Return, and some follow-up
replies.content: Yeah, all my planets are afflicted at least by aspect; unless you go with traditional Renaissance astrology and discount the modern planets, in which case my Sun is well placed (and still isn’t too badly off in any case - the afflicting aspect is a loose one, off by over eight degrees). I’d be happy to be a successful egomaniac, so maybe I should go for it.

replies.title: Re: Saturn Return, and some follow-up
replies.content:
By “it” I mean some solar magic, of course. I should have mentioned that.

Kevin

comments.title: Psychics for Hire
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

A friend and her adult daughter recently paid $50 each to sit in a psychic circle hosted by a local man who advertises in the monthly New Age paper. He’s well regarded, accordingl to my friend, who was quite impressed with him, as was her daughter, who he pegged as an “old soul.”

I don’t for a minute doubt that there are many realms of existence and people who can tap into them at will. But, the opportunity for fakery in this sort of psychic setup doesn’t inspire confidence, at least from me.

A long ago friend and counselor, who clearly had gifts in clairvoyance, used them discreetly and only as an adjunct to her practice. She was certain that those who use such gifts openly, and support themselves solely in this way, will get reap bad stuff once this life is done.

I’d appreciate knowing your views. Thank you so much for Magic Monday!

OtterGirl

replies.title: Re: Psychics for Hire
replies.content: There are some good professional psychics out there, but the odds of finding one aren't good, as they're hugely outnumbered by the scam artists -- and good psychics and scam artists alike are hugely outnumbered by enthusiastic and  clueless individuals who don't know any better. Still, your friend has to make her own choices, you know.

replies.title: Re: Psychics for Hire
replies.content:
Thank you! Yes, agreed, it’s my friend’s choice.

I’ve a bit of a callus on my tongue from biting it. Will recite my mother’s favorite saying regularly: One life per person.

comments.title: Burning the midnight oil
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,

Two questions from me this week.
Firstly, can you recommend any good books for beginners on interpreting/understanding astrology? I would like to get a better feel for all the terminology I read about as well as the added advantage of then being able to then ask better questions.

Second question. Is there some way of determining if a Tarot deck is suitable for divination practice if it is not on the approved list in Learning Ritual Magic? I understand that it is better to find a deck with artwork that resonates with the individual but I'm unsure how to determine its suitability. Like the "The Wild Unknown Tarot Deck" that is currently the best seller on Amazon.com. Would that deck be suitable for divination?

Thanks as always for providing this forum.

replies.title: Re: Burning the midnight oil
replies.content:
I'm going to toss the question of beginning astrology books to the readership. I haven't looked at beginner's books on the subject in many years, and have no idea what's out there.

With regard to tarot, if you're doing the work out of Learning Ritual Magic, you need to use one of the recommended decks for that work, period, end of sentence. If you're not doing the work out of Learning Ritual Magic, you can use any deck that appeals to you. I've seen people do effective divinations with decks that I find completely useless, so "suitable" means "suitable for the individual," and if the artwork and symbolism appeals to you, that's good enough.

replies.title: Intro Astrology Books
replies.content: On the subject of introductory astrology books, Chris Brennan posted a useful video last year (youtube.com/watch?v=7VKQi3Trqas), in which he recommends, among others: The Secret Language of Astrology by Roy Gillett; On the Heavenly Spheres by Helena Avelar and Luis Ribeiro; and The Essential Guide to Practical Astrology by April Elliott Kent. Any of these might be worth having a look at.

replies.title: Re: Burning the midnight oil
replies.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,
You responded to Marcu's request for a beginner's astrology book by referring it to the readership.
My recommendation is "The New Compleat Astrologer" by Derek and Julia Parker.
I just checked, and it is available thru Amazon, and probably also your local library and/or used bookstore.
Since this particular book was published in 1984, you may need to consult the ephemeris available thru astro.com, but (except for possibly outdated ephemerides) this book has *everything* to cast your own (or someone else's) horoscope, and interpret it.
Just follow the instructions one step at a time, and you will be fine.
I hope it helps.
Elizabeth Ann Kennett

comments.title: Silence
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
What are your thoughts on the role of silence in ones life?

Trevor

replies.title: Re: Silence
replies.content:
Other than the fact that there's rarely enough of it?

I'm not at all sure what you're asking here. Can you try to explain a little more clearly what you're looking for?

replies.title: Re: Silence
replies.content:
Thank you for your initial response.
I’m struggling with my own definition of silence, and what I hope to find there. I realize this sounds pretentious, but I’m honestly looking for something that doesn’t involve what the media tells me I should want, if that makes any sense.
Trevor

replies.title: Re: Silence
replies.content: How do you see this as relevant to magic and occultism? That's not a rhetorical question, by the way -- I'm trying to figure out exactly what you're asking, so my response won't be completely beside the point.

replies.title: Re: Silence
replies.content:
When you put it that way, no, I can’t see any connection with magic and occultism.
Therefore, I will give this some more thought and try and figure out just what questions I should be asking myself.
Thanks for your input.

Trevor

replies.title: Re: Silence
replies.content: Er, then why did you ask about it on a post that's specifically for questions about magic and occultism?

comments.title: Past life memory or channeling?
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,
Is there any way to tell if memories of another life are one's own past life or just information shared by a ghost?

I recently had an experience of hearing the song, "Go, Lassie Go," and wanted to learn to sing it. I brought up the lyrics and started to sing and began to sob. It is not a sad song but I have a limited empathic ability and the deep sorrow I felt was not my own. I also had a flash of understanding that this song reminded him of a beloved woman he had lost to the separation of death.

Also, a couple of sensitive friends have seen him at my house and he appears to be wearing a cape or, possibly, a cape-shouldered great coat. I fail to see how these could be examples of a past life memory of my own when he turns up here but another friend said he was me in another life and a ghost around my house at the same time as time does not exist.

He seems like a nice person and I am not afraid but puzzled. He seems very sad so I am trying to offer him friendship and love and I pray for him to stop taking himself so seriously.

I pray at an ancestor altar daily so this may have attracted him.
Max Rogers

replies.title: Re: Past life memory or channeling?
replies.content: That's a tough one. It could be either one. You might try asking the ghost if the memory is his.

replies.title: Re: Past life memory or channeling?
replies.content:
If it’s the one that starts out “Oh, the summertime is gone and the leaves are sweetly turning...” Rod Stewart wrote that, about 20-25 years ago. It’s on his album “A Spanner in the Works.”

—Fuzzy

replies.title: Re: Past life memory or channeling?
replies.content: No, he didn't write it, though he did a nice cover of it. Look it up in any reasonably comprehensive book of Scottish folk songs; it's titled either "Will Ye Go, Lassie, Go" or "Wild Mountain Thyme." It's at least a couple of centuries old.

replies.title: Re: Past life memory or channeling?
replies.content:
He’s got credit for writing it, at least on the album label; maybe he added a verse or two. The original composer isn’t likely to sue him!

I always did think that sounded like a folk song!

—Fuzzy

comments.title: Weather magic
comments.content:
Thank you for magic Monday.

On your encyclopaedia of natural magic you mention weather magic. Can you recommend any resources on it?

replies.title: Re: Weather magic
replies.content: There are scraps of material on weather magic in a lot of books on traditional folklore -- farm families have an obvious interest in the weather! -- but I don't know of a resource that focuses on it.

comments.title: House signs
comments.content:
Greetings from Down Under,

May I ask, which form of house division you use, and have you always used that one?
Also, have you had any experience with the sidereal Zodiac, or have any opinion about it?

Thank you for the opportunity to ask.

PS I am the proud owner of number 148 of "you know what" 😉
PSS plus many others!

And finally, my best wishes to Sara from a fellow crochet and cross stitcher.

replies.title: Re: House signs
replies.content:
I use Placidus these days; I've dabbled in a few others but find that using Placidus cusps reliably gives me better natal delineations and horary readings. I don't find the sidereal Zodiac useful at all -- to my mind, the entire sidereal system is based on a basic misunderstanding of the nature of astrology, though I know plenty of people would disagree.

Thank you, btw, and Sara sends her best in return!

comments.title: Learning Ritual Magic
comments.content:
Good morning JMG,

I have recently decided to seriously move past the dabbling stage and start working through Learning Ritual Magic. I am using the Golden Dawn Ritual Tarot as my deck, which has two different versions of the Temperance card. Do I need to use only one of these when I am going through the deck each day and for any later work with Temperance, and if so, is one version preferable to the other?

replies.title: Re: Learning Ritual Magic
replies.content: Yes, you should only use one of them for ordinary workings. The simpler one with the figure pouring water from vessel to vessel is the one to use; the other has a specific application to initiatory work, and is not meant for other uses.

replies.title: Re: Learning Ritual Magic
replies.content:
Thank you, I will proceed accordingly. A couple of related questions I thought of, is it okay to do the relaxation and attention exercises more than once a day? And if I do the attention exercise right after relaxation, should I do the opening and closing gesture separately for each of the two exercises, or is it okay to just open before relaxation and close after the attention exercise?

Finally, one question unrelated to the above and then I'll shush: did I read somewhere that you have an updated version of The Celtic Golden Dawn in the works, or did I just imagine that?

replies.title: Re: Learning Ritual Magic
replies.content:
Once per day is enough. Beginners very often want to pile into the work, and that's laudable, but it's not usually a good idea. Open and close separately for each practice.

As for The Celtic Golden Dawn, what's in process is a little more interesting -- well, at least to me! I've got a series of books in process that use the magical system I evolved for The Celtic Golden Dawn, one of which is already under contract with Llewellyn. My working guess is that I'll end up writing four or five books, each of which takes the CGD system of magic in a different direction, and all of which will be completely interworkable with the others.

comments.title: Free will and reincarnation
comments.content:
Hi John Michael,

We had a discussion on the previous Magic Monday about ‘free will’ and I agree with your interpretation, and my poor description was due to me being physically very tired. Anyway, I had a further thought bouncing on from that point and it is this: In order for reincarnation to work as it is generally understood, the small wiggle room for free will, that we discussed, is all a person can have access too. The two concepts are invariably linked. And it makes a nice sort of sense too in that context and works well.

Cheers

Chris

replies.title: Re: Free will and reincarnation
replies.content: Yep! It's precisely because we exist in a context of forces already in motion that our freedom is limited to "wiggle room." On the other hand, we can work to expand the wiggle room, and reach a point from which significant change in accordance with will becomes possible.

replies.title: Re: Free will and reincarnation
replies.content:
Thanks for the reply. Far out, busy with harvest and now we're back to infrastructure. I told you I have done something bad in a past life and am now busy redeeming myself.

Hope your spring is starting to bloom and that you and Sara are feeling the pleasing warmth of the renewed spring sun!

I guess your reply is the main game in town? :D

Chris

replies.title: Re: Free will and reincarnation
replies.content: Well, spring isn't here yet, and neither is the pleasing warmth; we're expecting 12 to 16 inches of snow overnight. Still, thank you!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I'm curious how important space is in ritual magic. On one hand most books recommend finding a quiet space that's not used for anything else, which makes sense, though isn't an option in my case. On the other hand there seems to be a considerable lore about wandering magicians, which would imply a set space is maybe not so necessary. I'm at a very early stage, just following the exercises in the first few chapters of your Learning Ritual Magic, but so far I've been doing everything outside, which seems to be working. Just curious if you have any thoughts on the role of a regular space to practice.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I've never had a space set apart for magical practice; I don't make enough money to afford that many rooms! For years I did my practices in the bedroom, which had the benefit of making nightmares a very rare experience for my wife as well as myself; in the last several places we've lived, I've had a study, and that functions as a magical space as well as a place for writing and for the books we don't necessarily want casual visitors to see. No doubt having a set space would be nice, but the absence of one hasn't slowed me down noticeably. I've done effective practices in hotel rooms, for that matter.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
AS my meditation practice continues with the nwyfre/qigong exercises I described previously, I've had some experiences with being able to "see" (sort of) my hands moving in spite of the fact that my eyes are closed during this particular portion of the ritual. What I "see" is a vague kind of photographic negative, with a luminous sometimes greenish sometimes purplish outline of my hands (which are quite warm at this point) against the dark background. I'm guessing that I'm seeing a portion of my etheric body? Occasionally, I have experiences of quick, bright flashes of light simultaneously in both corners of my side vision while this is all happening. Could you shed some light on what is occurring here?

Thanks!

David, by the lake

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yep. It's one of the standard forms of clairvoyance, and yes, you seem to be perceiving the etheric plane. You might try sometime seeing if you can perceive other people's auras the same way.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG, this may not be a question strictly about magic, but do you have resources to recommend about dealing with unresolved issues in one's inner life? By that I mean, without getting specific, I have over the past couple of years been regularly affected (every few weeks) by waves of thoughts and emotions about issues which I thought I had made decisions about and come to terms with. I guess the fact that these impulses have been appearing means that I haven't. :) It could be early midlife (I just finished reading Steppenwolf on your recommendation). I had a particularly bad bout of this last night while trying to sleep. Is this something that could be explored with psychology, and/or is it something that starting to learn magic may eventually be useful for? I just don't understand where these episodes come from and the fact they keep coming up means I have to find some way of understanding them.

PS - Ever since you mentioned the Raspberry Jam Law in one of your recent blog posts, toast with raspberry jam in the mornings has become a quite messy affair! :)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Journaling was something I used with good effect when I was dealing with similar issues. Get a blank notebook or something of that kind, sit down sometime when you can spare half an hour or so free from interruption, and let yourself start writing about the issues. Don't censor anything: whatever comes up, write it down. For me, at least, getting it all down on paper helped me get some distance from the emotional fog that tends to surround old issues, and in the process I was able to come to terms with a lot of things.

You might also look into psychology as a way of unpacking things. I'm partial to transactional analysis, and also to Albert Ellis' Rational-Emotive Therapy, as methods you can pick up pretty easily from books and self-study, which is a lot cheaper than going to a shrink.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you - I do journal already, but I censor myself to a certain extent, so I'll try as you suggest and stop doing that. I'll look for those resources you suggested, too.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's very often the habit of censoring our inner lives that keeps us from coming to terms with our past. A journal's a great way around that.

comments.title: Geomancy / garden blessings
comments.content:
If it is not too greedy to ask two questions, here they are:

#1: Sometimes I go through periods where I cast geomantic charts daily; currently I am in the midst of one such period. But this time there is a difference. I generate figures using ordinary playing cards (black = 2 points; red = 1 point) – quite often these days, when I am flipping the cards over to be face up I know the sequence of “reds & blacks”: this despite the fact that I keep my eyes closed while shuffling the cards and cutting the deck. I do not try to guess the sequence; it just comes to me. The question is whether this phenomenon is fairly common at a certain stage of divination practice.

#2: Try not to laugh (or get me committed to a psychiatric institution) but my backyard is being visited by the “Easter Bunny”. This apparently real rabbit first appeared in my backyard at dawn on Easter morning last year and then did not make an appearance again until last fall. The rabbit then decided to adopt my backyard all winter and has blessed it with an abundance of droppings – especially in the portion of my backyard which I have dedicated to wild beings. All this is in the context of me spending more time and conscious effort thanking the beings (physical and non-physical) who dwell in my backyard, and especially in the compost piles that flank the “wild area” of my garden. I take the position that my Easter Bunny incident is not “just a coincidence”. I guess that my question is whether it is common for those who pour positive intentions into their gardens to receive such blessings. Of course, if the bunny decides to adopt my backyard all year around and gorge itself on my organic vegetable gardens, I may not think it to be so much of a blessing!

Thanks again for sharing your encyclopaedic knowledge and extensive experience every Monday!

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Geomancy / garden blessings
replies.content:
What kind of psychism tends to surface in the early stages of practice varies significantly from person to person. The kind you're experiencing is one of the fairly common varieties. Do you "see" the red and black cards, or do you just "know" how they'll turn out? Those are two different modes.

As for your bunny. sometimes a rabbit is just a rabbit -- but not always. If the bunny vanishes when you plant your garden and doesn't return until fall, or if it stays around but limits its depredations to tolerable levels, then you may be dealing with an omen. Otherwise? Probably just a bunny.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy / garden blessings
replies.content: Thanks for the reply, JMG. On the first issue, I "know" what figures will turn out (e.g., one day, upon flipping the first card for the first mother, I knew that the 1st and 4th mothers would be Laetitia - which is what happened).

replies.title: Re: Geomancy / garden blessings
replies.content: Okay, then you've got common or garden variety heightened intuition. That can be immensely useful. I've used it to find occult bookstores in unfamiliar cities -- it just felt as though street X was the right street to turn down, and all of a sudden there I was. :-)

replies.title: Re: Geomancy / garden blessings
replies.content:
Good to know, JMG. Many thanks!

Ron M

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Apropos of last week's addendum, and reading of the comments on the referenced Galabes post, I had a question then that never quite got answered-too late in the month.

That is, simply, if the effective way to work with magic is focus on the end desired, how does one avoid means that are intolerable? If, say, one's desired result is a peaceful and harmonious home life, how does one use magic to further this goal without risking the dementia-stricken elder and obsteperous teenager's well being or even life? (I'm reminded of Bill Pulliam's guardian stories.)

BoysMom

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Good. You've realized one of the major downsides to magic. This is why broad vague magical goals ("a peaceful and harmonious home life") are best pursued with simple magical means such as natural magic; if, say, you use sachets and floor washes with herbs that traditionally bring peace and harmony, you're not likely to have any problem with unexpected consequences. Intensive magical workings are best aimed at very narrow, precisely chosen goals, so that you don't risk unintended consequences.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello John,
I was wondering if you could discuss the word Oghams. I'm not sure I grasp the concept of poetic metaphors. Thanks.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Think of the Robert Burns line, "My love is like a red, red rose." That's a poetic metaphor, and if Burns had the old bardic training and was into Ogham, he'd likely have used "a red, red rose" as a poetic metaphor for one of the Ogham fews. (I'll leave it as an exercise for you to decide which one.) Of course his love wasn't exactly like a red, red rose; I doubt she had roots, petals, or thorns, and if she had a green body and a bright red head she badly needed medical help! If you've ever seen a really intense red rose in the midst of green foliage, and noticed the way it visually "pops out" of the background, you might compare that to the way that the face of someone you love "pops out" of the background. That's the experience Burns was talking about, and the metaphor communicates that.

comments.title: Appropriate Magical Lodge rituals
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Inside a Magical Lodge was the first book I read by you. Reading the book inspired me to find women willing to form a group with me. Finally, after almost 6 years, four of us have been meeting monthly for about 9 months. In our meeting last night, we agreed that as a group, we are focusing our work on distilling plants into hydrosols and explore their effects on the material, etheric, astral, and mental levels. We would like to establish a ritual at the beginning of our meeting that creates an appropriate sacred space. Is the lesser pentagram ritual appropriate in this case? Are there other rituals you would recommend? While I have worked with the sphere of protection in the past, I have not worked with the lesser pentagram ritual.

Thank you,
Ann

replies.title: Re: Appropriate Magical Lodge rituals
replies.content: A lot depends on what deities you and the other members of your group want to invoke. The Pentagram rituals are very specific, and if you don't share a common set of deities, they can be awkward. I'd say, rather, that the Sphere of Protection is your best bet. It's very flexible, and does a good job of establishing sacred space.

replies.title: Re: Appropriate Magical Lodge rituals
replies.content: I was worried about the deity aspect so I am glad that the Sphere of Protection will work as I think that is something we can all agree on.  Should we each take an element to perform in the ritual or do we do everything in unison?  Does one of your books cover how to do it in a group setting?

replies.title: Re: Appropriate Magical Lodge rituals
replies.content:
There's a little discussion of that in my book The Druid Grove Handbook. I wouldn't do it in unison; you could certainly have each person do one element, or one person could do the ritual each meeting, and change who does it from meeting to meeting.

There's a very simple opening and closing ritual you can do which blends well with the Sphere of Protection -- unsurprisingly, because it was created by Juliet Ashley, who also came up with the SoP. To do this, you need a central altar on which are symbols of the four elements; a lit stick of incense, a lit candle in a holder, a cup or bowl of water, and a large stone or a bowl of earth are suitable. (These are placed east, south, west, and north respectively.) There are four chairs, one in each quarter, facing inward, and one participant sits in each.

The person leading the ritual, who can be any of the members, says: "Sisters, please attend as we begin our work. Sister (name), please perform the Sphere of Protection." (She does so, doing the invocations and banishings outside the circle of chairs, and everyone else visualizes the symbols and energies along with her.)

The person leading the ritual then says, "Let us bless this temple and all within it by the four elements."

The person in the east stands up, goes to the altar, and picks up the incense, saying, "I bless this temple and all within it with the element of air." She circles the altar inside the circle of chairs, going from east to east, and then returns the incense to the altar and sits down.

The person in the south then stands up, goes to the altar, and picks up the candle, saying, "I bless this temple and all within it with the element of fire." She circles the altar inside the circle of chairs, going from south to south, and then returns the candle to the altar and sits down.

The person in the west then does the same with the cup of water. Finally, the person in the north does the same with the stone or bowl of earth.

The person leading the ritual then says, "By the power of the elements our work is begun."

For the closing ritual, leave out the SoP, do the elemental blessings the same way, and say "...as we complete our work" at the beginning and "...our work is complete" at the end.

It's simple, clean, straightforward, easy to do, and it adds a very definite energy to the meeting. Consider giving it a try.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: How do you make an object unnoticeable?

replies.title: Unnoticeable Object
replies.content:
Put it in a roomful of people staring at smartphones. 😄

(Is anyone else tired of having to dodge smartphone addicts to avoid being knocked down?)

A person from Los Angeles told me that you can walk right past a celebrity and not notice, they’re very good at rendering themselves inconspicuous. I also remember reading an anecdote by a man walkng on a crowded street with Norma Jean Baker. “Watch this,” says Norma Jean, and before his eyes she transforms herself into Marilyn Monroe. There is candid footage of her still extant, so if you could learn her trick and then reverse it, you’d be on the right track.

—Fuzzy

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You've got two choices.

a) Lock it in a closet.

b) Devote the next ten years to systematic daily practice of ritual magic, until you've reached a level of skill that will enable you to do things like that.

A lot of people think of magic the way they think of technology, and treat it as though it's plug-and-play -- as though, with no previous training, they can just pick up a spell, do it, and get results. It doesn't work that way. You might as well ask a martial artist what technique will win a fight; it's not the technique that wins the fight, it's the individual who's reached a sufficient level of training, strength, and toughness to win a fight. If you want to make objects unnoticeable through magic, you have a helluva lot of work ahead of you.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Thanks as always for doing this, JMG. Yesterday morning, a beautiful adult osprey perched on a tree directly outside of my front door (my son spotted him first)... Ospreys are not at all an unusual site for Florida, but I've never seen one actually in our neighborhood before (we're in a fairly urban setting). He rested for several minutes, gave us quite the song and unfurled his wings for us long enough for my kids to grab their binoculars and me to grab my camera and get some fantastic pics. We all took a lot of joy from his visit. Later, while I was looking at the pics, I got the distinct impression that, well, there was something more to the osprey's visit... that he was an omen? Or a messenger? Not being well-versed in augury, I thought I'd ask if you had any thoughts on a good interpretation, or if you might point me to some resources to consult? Thanks again!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'm not at all familiar with the resources on augury; it's not something that the modern occult tradition uses much. Perhaps someone else reading this has something to suggest.

replies.title: Augury
replies.content:
Augury is part of the broader class of divination involving reading omens and portents. I’m sure there are resources out there, you just have to do some research. The very basic level is to write a list of all the birds in your area and look up the symbolism of each bird. This is very simplistic. You also need to know the symbolism of specific bird behaviors (nesting, capturing prey, flying east vs west, one bird vs five birds, etc.) and it gets more involved from there. I never bothered with all those details, though.

I learned to read omens and portents on accident. I was trying to learn dream symbolism. I put a couple years into studying my dreams. I used “The Everything Dreams Book” but you can use any book that has one section devoted to the symbolism itself (for example, water symbolizes emotions) and another section devoted to how-to (how to remember dreams, how to find your personal symbolism, etc.).

Once you can interpret dreams, you can use these skills to interpret your waking reality as if it were a dream. Read a bit on synchronicity and that will tell you *when* to interpret reality like a dream. That’s everything you need to know (and where I stopped studying it) but then pouring over elaborate bird symbolism and such will take your studies to the next level. I find the study of symbolism will aid any divination system and any ritual working, plus it adds extra depth to the arts (especially poetry and visual art). It’s an intense course of study, though. You won’t master it over the course of a week.

Jessi Thompson
anotheramethyst

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I know nothing about augury as such, but I did learn something startling about its roots from William James Burroughs in _Climate Change in Prehistory: The End of the Reign of Chaos_. Burroughs uses ice cores and other data to argue that Earth's climate didn't stabilize enough to justify agriculture until the Holocene.

Our paleolithic ancestors had no weather satellites, but they had good eyes. Initially, paying attention to a sudden flush of birds might alert one to the presence of an ambush predator. Reflective observers then took note of migratory birds as markers for forthcoming weather changes and seasonal events like the timing of game migrations. Burroughs specifically discusses this: bird migrations gave ancient humans a window on large-scale environmental processes with life-and-death local impact. By the time Rome had an official augur, humans had been watching birds for divination for *a long time*.

You say your osprey is not unusual for Florida, but he is unusual for an urban setting. The Weather Underground blog folk have been discussing the unusual character of the train of near-record nor'easters this year. Again, I know nothing of augury, but I myself would take such a visit as a low-hint that the line between urban and not-urban is thinning as a result of larger forces in play.

comments.title: Counteracting parsley?
comments.content: A few Mondays ago you mentioned parsley attracts spirits of the dead. For someone looking to grow parsley strictly for culinary use should any action be taken? Plant next to X to balance the influences? Leave a small food offering under the plant periodically? Is it worth worrying about for non-mages?

replies.title: Re: Counteracting parsley?
replies.content:
I can't say for sure about spirits of the dead (I'm not very sensitive to them) but one thing parsley definitely attracts down here in Florida is swallowtail butterflies. Not only does parsley (and also dill and rue) attract the butterflies, but they'll lay their eggs on them, and the funky colored caterpillars will eat off the leaves and softer stems. Plant extra if you see a bunch of butterflies. Also, if starting by seed, parsley seeds take forever to sprout (like up to 6 weeks). Companion planting says parsley will add vigor to tomatoes or asparagus, and is considered helpful to repelling flies from carrots and beetles from roses.

*I am researching (mundane) companion planting for herbs right now.

dfr

replies.title: Re: Counteracting parsley?
replies.content: Don't worry about it. Lots of people grow parsley without being inconvenienced by ghosts; as long as you're not doing sensitive magical operations, it shouldn't be a problem.

comments.title: Question about television
comments.content:
I've noticed for a while now that people who do not watch television are, by and large, more successful than people who don't. This seems to be true even comparing people who watch it for an hour or so a week versus those who don't at all.

Do you know of anything that might explain this, beyond the time people waste on it?

replies.title: Re: Question about television
replies.content: The time-wasting effect is pretty considerable, when you realize that most Americans waste around four hours a day watching television, but I think you're right that there's more to it than that. There was research a while back suggesting that when people watch TV, the flicker of the screen entrains their brain waves into an alpha rhythm, which is the rhythm of shallow trance; people whose brain waves are in an alpha state absorb information uncritically and unthinkingly. If that's true, television makes you stupid by filling your brain with nonsense and keeping you from thinking clearly about it.

comments.title: Discursive meditation for deep learning
comments.content: Hello John. Could discursive meditation be used to improve depth of learning for non-occult materials? If not, can you recommend any good technique for this purpose? Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Discursive meditation for deep learning
replies.content: I'd say give it a try. The other thing to try is close reading: the way of reading in which you read one sentence at a time, and think about it before you go to the next sentence. That's one of the great forgotten skills of learning nowadays.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
The focus on sensation has been interesting. I notice that my imagination in magical workings is far more tactile than visual, which surprises me, as that is strongly contrary to my self-description. For instance vibration, moving it into my fingers, or into the pentagons, and other imagined forms, is much more vivid that any visual type impression. Meditating I find that there are extrodinary ticklish rushes of sensation, particullarly in my forarms when my 'pores' breath air; and considerable rushes of sensation around my pelvis.

Focus is improving and becoming more pleasant in meditation, and I am getting ready for moving to meditation 6 shortly; but a question. As written it seems that on each occassion one only does a single '7in 7out cycle' before the meditation; each meditation rotating to a subsuquent element as listed. It strikes me a strange not to continue with the 7 fold repetitions. Am I understanding these directions correctly?

I don't suppose verbena hastata is an acceptable substatute for officinalis?

Ray Wharton

replies.title: Verbena
replies.content:
If I may, Ray (and JMG)...

The herbalist Matthew Wood (whose knowledge and expertise I esteem very highly) says in his Earthwise Herbal Repertory that the two Verbenas (V. hastata and V. offcinalis) can be used interchangeably.

That said, what are you trying to accomplish with them? It's unclear, to me at least, whether this was supposed to be connected to your other questions or not.

I've personally worked closely with Blue Vervain (Verbena hastata), but not with Verbana off. at all. Based on my direct experiences and study, and some conversations with others, Verbena hastata has a very powerful effect of bringing me "into my body," as it were, as opposed to being preoccupied in more abstract realms of the mind; the herb is standardly discussed along these lines.

That said, this effect can be intense and overpowering, especially for those on the autism spectrum who already have a heightened tactile sensitivity.** Verbena, even at very small doses, can make easily take those sensitivities from "heightened but quite familiar and tolerable" to "hyperaware of every part of my skin" to "painful" or even "unbearable." (I'm curious, JMG, if you or anyone else you know with Aspergers has reported anything like this. Likewise Violet or any other herbalists who may be reading.)

I mention this here partly because of your discussions of tactile magical perception above, though it may be a totally unrelated point.

--barefootwisdom


**This would probably also apply to those with hypersensitivity in another sensory modality, though I don't have any firsthand experience to draw from here.

replies.title: Re: Verbena
replies.content:
This is very interesting information, thank you. The reason I am asking JMG has to do with particular details of the CGD. I am confident that from a purely herbal perspective they are relativly interchangable, but whether they are interchangable from a specifically magical context is beyond my reckoning. I have a garden spot that would really welcome some blue verbena, and better access to local seeds and wild crafting.


Ray Wharton

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Nope. The two kinds of verbena have similar medical properties but very different magical properties.

comments.title: Love and Loss
comments.content: In my copy of the Celtic Golden Dawn, the Bardic grade material on Geomancy says that, for questions regarding love, Colled indicates false love, Elw true love. Should this be reversed, given that the Ovate material has Colled as favorable in questions of love, and that, as Amissio, Colled is traditionally related to Venus?

replies.title: Re: Love and Loss
replies.content:
Reflecting on Amissio, the relevant notion here might be "losing one's heart (to the other)." With that in mind, I can see how it might have associations of falling head over heels into something shallow that ultimately won't last. So that might be "false love" as opposed to a deep, abiding, less glamorous but utimately more fulfilling "true love" (with notions of fulfillment and "hanging on to it" in the long term perhaps associating with Acquisitio).

At any rate, as one geomancer to another, thank you for something interesting to take to meditation in the next few days!

--barefootwisdom

replies.title: Re: Love and Loss
replies.content: No, it should not be reversed. One thing every student of occultism needs to learn to get used to is that there are always inconsistencies in the material, and those are not there by accident. For the time being, I'd encourage you to keep practicing geomancy, pay attention to readings in which Colled appears, and draw your own conclusions from the results of your divinations.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I got an email about Equinox Cracks in Earth's magnetic field from spaceweather.com and started wondering what effect this might have on magical workings on these days. I was especially intrigued by the last line of the partial article below:

"EQUINOX CRACKS IN EARTH'S MAGNETIC FIELD: The vernal equinox is less than 10 days away. That means one thing: Cracks are opening in Earth's magnetic field. Researchers have long known that during weeks around equinoxes fissures form in Earth's magnetosphere. Solar wind can pour through the gaps to fuel bright displays of Arctic lights. One such episode occurred on March 9th.

This is called the the "Russell-McPherron effect," named after the researchers who first explained it. The cracks are opened by the solar wind itself. South-pointing magnetic fields inside the solar wind oppose Earth's north-pointing magnetic field. The two, N vs. S, partially cancel one another, weakening our planet's magnetic defenses. This cancellation can happen at any time of year, but it happens with greatest effect around the equinoxes. Indeed, a 75-year study shows that March is the most geomagnetically active month of the year, followed closely by September-October--a direct result of "equinox cracks."

NASA and European spacecraft have been detecting these cracks for years. Small ones are about the size of California, and many are wider than the entire planet. While the cracks are open, magnetic fields on Earth are connected to those on the sun. Theoretically, it would be possible to pick a magnetic field line on terra firma and follow it all the way back to the solar surface."
http://www.spaceweather.com

I'd love to know your thoughts on this. Thanks

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The equinoxes are important periods for certain kinds of magical workings. In the Golden Dawn tradition, the time right around the equinoxes opens a window in which the magical energies of the sun and the earth come into closer contact than at any other time of year. Hmm -- I wonder what that sounds like?

comments.title: seeing lights
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

After staring at my hands for a short while, with strongly fixed attention, it's like they start to glow. I eventually see an intense aquamarine light surrounding them. If I then move my hand away, I see a vivid afterimage in the same color.

Just as a side note, by practicing trying to induce this effect over quite a long period, I've gotten noticeably better at it. Mostly I can make it happen much faster. It's a trick of how you use your awareness, like a lot of things.

Is this anything "occult" or am I just experiencing some sort of visual receptor fatigue phenomenon? Aquamarine is my favorite color, which makes this quite a coincidence if this is all just mundane.

Thanks,

Warp

P.S. When I was super-exhausted from lack of sleep due to a back injury, while sitting in a big recliner chair in a kind of trance, I started to notice that I was seeing something even though my eyes were closed. After some attention work I managed to stabilize it and it seemed to be an image of the room from my perspective, but it was in black and white instead of color and it was...shrunken. Like taking a normal perspective, in 3D, and moving it forward into the horizon so that it recedes and appears smaller. Around the image was blackness.

I kinda felt like I was floating above my body a bit during this, btw.

Was that the etheric?

Also, a friend has seen through his eyelids in a state of trance, he could see almost normally, but everything was colored red, like seeing through a red filter. Was that the etheric?

replies.title: Re: seeing lights
replies.content: It could be the etheric; it could also be something else. I wasn't there at the time, so can't vouch for it either way!

comments.title: Nuclear Power & Magic
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,

what are the metaphysical implications of nuclear power? On September 27, 2016 a user named Brigyn asked that question on your Well of Galabes blog. I haven't found your answer to that question, but I am really intrigued by the idea. Does nuclear power create pollution on more than just the physical level?

replies.title: Re: Nuclear Power & Magic
replies.content: Some occult philosophies suggest so. I haven't really studied the matter in great detail, though.

comments.title: Magic and Cats
comments.content:
Kind of a fun one.

One of my cats nearly always join me when I practice magic. If she doesn't turn up during the pentagram ritual I feel her pawing about my feet while I'm standing for the Central Ray.

One time we had guests, and I was practicing magic in a different room from usual, with the door closed. While beginning the Central Ray, I heard my cat scratching at the door. She is very persistent and very attached to me; often enough if she can't get into a room she wants to be in she starts trying to dig through the carpet, with the result that there is no way we're ever getting our security deposit back. Any way, I didn't want to stop my practice to let her in, so I imagined some of the light from the Central Ray as it burst over my head filtering out through the carpet and under the door, where my cat could lap it up like a bowl of milk. The scratching and yowling stopped, and when I finished my practice I found her asleep next to the door outside the room.

So my question...

Are you aware of any information on the long-term effect of ceremonial magic of this sort on animals? Has anyone done any work on this topic, or have you noticed anything?

replies.title: Re: Magic and Cats
replies.content: When we had a cat, she would inevitably respond to my setting up my altar for magical working by running into the room, curling up under the altar and going to sleep for the duration of the working. It didn't seem to hurt her any; she lived to the ripe old age of nineteen. I've never heard of anyone studying it systematically, but I've heard many such stories from other occultists, and the cats apparently thrive on it.

replies.title: Re: Magic and Cats
replies.content:
I have a favorite oak tree outside, under which I like to practice my SoP. One of my cats loves to come along, climb up the tree, and pace along a low horizontal limb while I'm practicing. I've never seen him interested in the tree at other times.
--Heather in CA

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content:
Hello. I have a copy of your book The Art and Practice of Geomancy. I have been working with it for more than a year. I feel like I am making progress with getting to know the oracle, and have received valuable and unexpected insights from it. However it seems like my skill has plateaued in recent months. Is there anyone teaching geomancy that you recommend? I would like to study with someone if it is available.
Thanks,
Goats and Roses

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: A plateau is normally what happens for a few months before you break through to the next level. No, I don't know of anybody who teaches geomancy, and I don't know what a teacher could do for you that you can't do just as well for yourself by studying your readings and systematically comparing them to what actually happened.

comments.title: Imaginary Friends
comments.content:
What is the general occultist understanding of the phenomenon of the imaginary friend? I have no conscious recollection of this, but my mother tells me that when I was 2-3 years old, I had an imaginary friend who would accompany me, who I would play with. He had a name. We lived in Germany at the time. Was this a spirit, a ghost of some sort, a guardian, or perhaps any of the above? I have tried to contact him recently, and receive the distinct message that I don't need him anymore.
And a corollary to this, do entities come into and go out of our lives depending on need, circumstances, and openness to the experience? And would this apply to deities as well?

Thank you,
Yanocoches in Colorado

replies.title: Re: Imaginary Friends
replies.content: I don't know. I haven't seen anyone discuss this in the occult literature. As for your corollary, well, why do people come and go in your life?

replies.title: Re: Imaginary Friends
replies.content:
My mother tells me that when she was quite small she had two invisible companions that she would play with. When something moved her mother to tell her that she had had twin older sisters who died shortly after birth her invisible companions stopped coming to her. She has always felt that they just wanted her to know that they had existed.

My grandmother told me that she was greatly comforted by a vision that appeared in the door of the room in which she was recovering from the birth--a vision of Jesus holding her babies. My grandmother fit Cardinal Newman's description of the English as people who read the Bible and believed in Providence, as she had little use for preachers or churches, but did read the Bible and believe that God would take care of her.

Rita

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
How would you choose a tradition to work in, what criteria would you use, if you had to do that all over again?

I find myself drawn to druidry and Feri witchcraft. I don't see why I couldn't eventually work at both, but right now I need to work at one so I'm grounded somewhere before crossing wires.

I'm not a stranger to magical or spiritual practice, but up til now I've been going at things in a very eclectic/chaos magic way. I'm coming up on a Saturn return in the next couple of years, so I think the Old Goat's nudging me the way of more formal structure.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'd read extensively in the traditions that seemed attractive to me, and then pick one -- if necessary, by flipping a coin -- knowing that what practices you do matters a lot less than the simple fact of doing the practices. I'd also pay attention the people I know who've studied the traditions I'm considering, knowing that whatever character qualities they have in common, I'll probably pick up in the course of training.

comments.title: sibilants
comments.content:
Hello there JMG,

My question is about vibration and letter-sounds.

I've noticed since I've been paying closer attention to it that within a word being vibrated, some sounds do indeed buzz and vibrate more or less than others, and some slip by like water.

I am focusing on feeling the vibration in various parts of my body, practicing getting more sensitive to and strengthening that. Certainly all the letter sounds have their own special qualities that I can feel more or less distinctly. The sibilants, however, I can hardy feel outside of my head at all. The ssss and ssshhh sounds seem to flow by at a different speed, or having a different quality of resistance than the others. That particular quality makes it much more difficult for me to feel that sound in my hand or in any other specific bodily location than the others. The sibilants feel less like I'm making something concrete than like a "skip" in the density of the vibration, if that makes sense.

Names that begin with "S"- I don't feel like I've really begun to vibrate the name until I begin to pronounce the next letter, which feels weird to me. "H"s too. (It never felt weird when I was paying less detailed attention to it, but now that my observatioal magnification has been dialed up...it's weird.)

Is this something that with practice will get to feel more even and less lopsided over time? Are these sounds handled differently?

I'm hoping that my sensitivity to these sounds will increase with practice, and then I'll know more about how they behave vibrationally. Curious about your take/experiences.

As always, many thanks!
Bonnie

replies.title: Re: sibilants
replies.content: The sibilants have a very different quality, and it takes more practice to get an obvious effect -- or rather to perceive the effect that's been there the whole time. Keep at it and you'll get there!

comments.title: Influence
comments.content: Relative novice here...can you suggest what ritual(s) I should use to keep myself free from undue etheric influence from another person? I am doing a variant of the Elemental Cross I found on a Christian occult site (God above me to watch over me, below me to support, on my right to counsel, on my left to guide, behind to protect, in front to lead, within to speak) and the Sphere of Protection. Is it time to introduce calling and banishing the elements, or is there something else altogether that I should be doing to shield from the other's emotional influence? Many thanks!!

replies.title: Re: Influence
replies.content: Bring in the calling and banishing of the elements, and include undue etheric influence as one of the things you banish by the elemental powers. If you prefer to use Christian symbolism, remember that you can invoke archangels at the four quarters -- traditionally, Raphael in the east, Michael in the south, Gabriel in the west, and Uriel in the north. If you're comfortable praying to the Virgin Mary, the usual scheme is the Virgin Mary for Spirit Below, the risen Christ for Spirit Above, and the Holy Spirit for Spirit Within.

comments.title: SoP elemental symbols
comments.content:
Greetings JMG,

What is the origin of the elemental symbols used in the SoP for Air and Earth, and are they AODA-specific? Obviously the triangles for Water and Fire are used in many other contexts, but I've not run across the circle and line anywhere but the SoP, and I'm wondering whether they're AODA-specific or whether I've just missed them in other sources.

In Dr. Gilbert's article on the SoP from the AODA website, I see "The ancient symbol for the Air Element is a circle with a vertical line extending above it." Meaning no disrespect to either possible mode of "ancient," I suppose I'm wondering whether this refers to the sort that comes of decades/centuries of use, or the sort that extends back to three Tuesdays ago, so to speak, when a more unicursal element symbol was called for.

Thanks and best wishes,
Jim

replies.title: Re: SoP elemental symbols
replies.content:
I believe they were invented by Juliet Ashley, the third Grand Archdruid of AODA and the creator of much of the order's current symbolism. She was active in the 1950s and 1960s, so there have been the necessary decades of use.

They're not unique to AODA because Dr. Ashley also ran half a dozen other occult orders, which used similar symbolism. As far as I know those orders are currently in abeyance, but there may be some initiates around who still practice those systems; for that matter, I was initiated into all of them in the course of becoming Grand Archdruid of AODA, and may do something with one or more of them in due time.

comments.title: Re: Fencing under a full moon...
comments.content:
It was an experiment of mine a while ago; I don't currently practice the system of occult themed swordsmanship it teaches, but it's perfectly viable. To rework it for Celtic Golden Dawn use -- hmm. That's actually quite easy, as all you'd have to do is swap out the Hebrew terms for Welsh ones. The exercises and sword drills remain unchanged.

As for the scientists, have they shown that there's an overload of positively charged ions in the atmosphere at the time of the full moon? Or is it just one more ad hoc hypothesis?

comments.title: Spiritless Sphere of Protection?
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

In the past you have commented on the relative safety of the practices described in The Druid Magic Handbook when done in a household with small children, and I found this very reassuring. However, more recently you wrote that “Anything that uses ceremonial means to call on anything other than a divinity should be avoided around children until they’re past their seventh birthday”. Yet in the Druid Magic Handbook, your version of the Sphere of Protection summons each element and it’s gods, as well as it’s spirits and powers. Furthermore, the instructions for scrying describe a way to make contact with beings which are not understood as divinities.

Should I avoid summoning any “spirits and powers” during the SOP, as well as any work with guides in the imaginal realm, if I I wish to avoid drawing unwelcome attention to my children?

Best regards, and thank you for any clarification.

replies.title: Re: Spiritless Sphere of Protection?
replies.content:
You can do the Sphere of Protection and its related practices quite safely among children -- I know quite a few people who've done so. When you call on the gods, spirits, and powers of the elements in the SoP, you're not calling on individual spirits by name, you're just acknowledging the entire realm of elemental spirits and powers. (That is to say, the comment of mine you quoted was not as exact as it should have been.)

Scrying is always safe for bystanders -- you're going to the spirits, not bringing them into your home! The thing you don't want to do is any working that focuses on bringing nondivine spirits into manifestation where you are: for example, ceremonial evocation of individual spirits.

comments.title: Tarot Reversals
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

Thanks for your earlier essay on Tarot, which is a great resource. I have been working through the daily practice, but I am confused about the meaning of reversals. Can you suggest a framework for helping to clarify this?

Many thanks,

Samurai_47

replies.title: Re: Tarot Reversals
replies.content: Don't look for frameworks or formulas. If you're using the Little White Book that came with your deck as your jumping-off point for meanings, simply take the reversed meaning it gives as your starting place, and learn what the reversed cards mean to you the same way you're learning what the cards mean upright: by comparing your daily readings to the events of the day, and seeing which events seem to link up to which cards.

replies.title: Re: Tarot Reversals
replies.content: Your response really rings true. I have been getting so caught up in conflicting methods for interpreting reversals that I might have been missing the point, which is to develop an intuitive feel. Very helpful!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Thank you for this weekly offering! It's been a while since I commented, but I have been following along with delight.

My question is about scrying and its relation to active imagination in Jung's sense. A common suggestion is to begin an active imagination session by reconstructing a dream you'd like to work with-- essentially, scrying it and seeing what follows.

I know it is bad practice to scry actual living people. On the other hand, meaningful dreams very often feature people you know (at least, mine do).

Is there a recommended approach here, something analogous to the elemental sign test? Perhaps a method of inviting those figures to wear a different face, or reveal their difference from the literal person?

Stuart

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You'd probably have to talk to somebody who has experience with Jungian methods. In occult practice scrying is used almost exclusively to unpack the meanings of traditional symbols -- you don't use it on your dreams, you use it on Tarot trumps, alchemical emblems, and the like -- so there are no guidelines for using it the way you've described.
